channel.title: Bloomberg Green Global
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Bloomberg Green Global

item.title: What Starmer’s Resignation Means for the UK’s Climate Goals
item.description:
London’s climate week began today with big political news: UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s decision to resign. One area where his successor could make concessions to appease the opposition is on fossil fuels.

Today’s newsletter looks at prospects for the UK’s world-leading climate goals in the post-Starmer era. Meanwhile, business leaders, politicians and experts convening in London to discuss global warming are battling scorching temperatures as a heat wave sweeps across Europe.

Boiling climate week

By Jess Shankleman, Olivia Rudgard, and Akshat Rathi

London Climate Action Week is kicking off in one of the last major economies still pushing hard and fast toward net-zero emissions. But in the wake of Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s planned resignation, even the UK’s commitment is looking shakier.

While nations from the US and Canada to Germany and India are scaling back plans to cut planet-warming gases, the UK this month unveiled one of the world’s most ambitious strategies to keep long-term global warming below 1.5C, a goal that almost 200 countries signed up to in 2015. The UK’s new 2040 target — which still needs to be approved by Parliament — would reduce greenhouse gases by 87% from 1990 levels.

“The long-term plan makes sense for both economic and security reasons,” said UK Climate Minister Katie White on the sidelines of London Climate Action Week, with temperatures in the city expected to reach 39C (102F) in the coming days. “We are increasingly vulnerable to the international fossil-fuel market and people can see that.” Every pound spent on reaching net zero will benefit the economy by at least twice that amount, according to the Climate Change Committee, the government’s independent adviser.

The Labour government faces significant pressure to make fossil-fuel policy concessions that would put its goal at risk. It has stood firm, so far.

Energy Secretary Ed Miliband has resisted calls from various corners of business and politics to allow new drilling licenses in the North Sea. He faces powerful critics including US President Donald Trump and RenewableUK, a professional association that says many of its members service both the traditional energy sector and the newer, cleaner one.

But the government’s ongoing commitment to cutting emissions is in question, following Starmer’s announcement on Monday.

Worsening economic prospects and some of the world’s highest energy prices will be key issues for Starmer’s successor. Andy Burnham, who announced his bid for the Labour leadership on Monday, has said he is “open-minded” about the prospect of more drilling in British waters.

Meanwhile, Nigel Farage’s poll-leading Reform UK has said it would ditch climate goals, cancel planned wind-farm contracts and drill for more oil and gas. And the opposition Conservative Party has vowed to get rid of the Climate Change Act, which underpins the UK’s net-zero targets, if it wins the next election. Last week, the Conservatives won its first Westminster by-election in Scotland for decades, a victory that party leader Kemi Badenoch framed as a broader verdict on Labour’s energy policies.

Other countries are exploiting their fossil-fuel reserves even as they deploy more emission-avoiding renewables, according to Wafa Jafri, partner and UK head of energy strategy at KPMG. Examples include Australia and China.

“The UK is not isolated in its direction but could look isolated in the pace and ambition it has set,” she said. “Others are taking a pragmatic approach — expanding renewables where they can, while maintaining fossil-fuel security where they must.” Miliband risks losing support among British households and businesses struggling with the cost of energy, she added.

Extreme heat

39CTemperature that London could hit in the coming days.

Hitting home

“We’re already seeing the impacts of climate change and it focuses the mind” Katie White UK Climate Minister

Europe bakes

By Joe Wertz, Nayla Razzouk, and Eva Brendel

A heat wave gripping western Europe is forecast to strengthen this week, with days of dangerously hot and humid conditions and record-breaking temperatures in France, the UK, Spain and Germany.

The heat — driven by a high-pressure heat dome and supported by a developing El Niño — has caused widespread disruptions, with school closures in France and a World Cup viewing event canceled in Spain. A surge in cooling demand has also driven up power prices.

Forecasts for unusually warm temperatures continue through July, making this only an early summer test for the world’s fastest-warming continent.

“There is a good chance we will see more rounds of heat this summer,” said Jason Nicholls, the lead international forecaster for AccuWeather.

Temperature records were shattered in several cities across France on Sunday, and the highest level recorded for the day was 42.2C (108F) in Pissos in the southwest of the country, according to government forecaster Météo-France.

French authorities have mobilized about 250,000 firefighters across the country to help with heat-related calls and issued a red alert for 49 departments covering Paris and much of the western and southwestern parts of the country, with daytime highs of 42C expected until Thursday, forecasters said.

The persistent heat wave will test Europe’s energy grid. Record-high power demand for cooling is expected, according to analysis from Vaisala and Commodity Weather Group.

At the same time, French nuclear output is facing production restrictions to comply with environmental rules. Electricite de France said its Golfech 2 nuclear reactor will be halted from 11:45 p.m. Monday through June 30 due to high temperatures on the Garonne River, and warned that other plants may have to curb generation this week.

Global warming is expensive

What Climate Change Costs You at the CheckoutHarsher Weather Is Coming for India’s Health Insurance BillsEngland’s Critical Infrastructure Upgrades Bring Steep Water Bills

Your Zero listen

The key to tackling climate change is moving away from burning fossil fuels to using renewable energy. But in his book More and More and More, French historian Jean-Baptiste Fressoz argues that the world has never actually managed a successful energy transition before and current plans are unrealistic. Many have taken his writing to mean that stopping global warming is impossible, however, he tells Akshat Rathi this week on Zero, his view is actually quite different.

Listen now, and subscribe on Apple, Spotify or YouTube to get new episodes of Zero every Thursday.

More from Bloomberg

  • Business of Food for a weekly look at how the world feeds itself in a changing economy and climate, from farming to supply chains to consumer trends
  • Energy Daily for a daily guide to the energy and commodities markets that power the global economy
  • Tech In Depth for analysis and scoops about the business of technology
item.dc:creator: Jess Shankleman, Olivia Rudgard, Akshat Rathi, Joe Wertz, Nayla Razzouk, Eva Brendel

item.title: Heat Wave Shows Why French Reactors Must Adapt to Climate Change
item.description: Global warming and extreme weather are forcing French nuclear reactors to adapt.
item.dc:creator: Joe Wertz

item.title: Octopus Energy, CATL Announce Battery-Swapping Hubs for Trucks
item.description: UK energy supplier Octopus Energy Group Ltd. and Chinese battery manufacturer Contemporary Amperex Technology Co., or CATL, will build a European network of battery-swapping hubs for trucks, the two companies said on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Olivia Rudgard, Akshat Rathi

item.title: UK’s World-Leading Climate Goals Look Shakier in Post-Starmer Era
item.description: The British government faces significant pressure to make policy concessions on fossil fuels. It has stood firm, so far.
item.dc:creator: Jess Shankleman, Olivia Rudgard, Akshat Rathi

item.title: Europe Heat Wave Sends Power Prices Soaring as Alerts Spread
item.description: A heat wave gripping western Europe is forecast to strengthen this week, with days of dangerously hot and humid conditions and record-breaking temperatures in France, the UK, Spain and Germany.
item.dc:creator: Joe Wertz, Nayla Razzouk, Eva Brendel

item.title: Twelve EU Nations Push to Boost Carbon Fund for Poorer States
item.description: A group of 12 European Union countries urged the bloc’s executive to bolster a fund financed through the carbon market which supports poorer member states in the transition away from fossil fuels.
item.dc:creator: Ewa Krukowska

item.title: What Climate Change Costs You at the Checkout
item.description: The higher temperatures climb, the more inflated household costs like groceries will become, economists predict.
item.dc:creator: Emma Court, Kyle Kim

item.title: Red Weather Warning Issued in UK for Extreme Heat and Humidity
item.description: Red weather warning issued for extreme heat and humidityBy Emily Beament and Rebecca Speare-Cole, Press Association
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Climate Fund Guarantees Set to Woo $5 Billion for Mexico, Brazil
item.description: The Climate Investment Funds, a $14 billion pool of climate-focused capital, said it has approved funding for Brazil and Mexico that should help unlock billions of dollars in additional financing to speed up the decarbonization of their industrial sectors.
item.dc:creator: Antony Sguazzin

item.title: Iran War Puts Bangladesh’s Industrial Dreams at Risk
item.description: Inside a new Bangladeshi glass factory with a production floor nearly twice the size of a soccer field, machinery sits wrapped in tarpaulins. At a neighboring steel mill, construction has slowed and production has yet to begin.
item.dc:creator: Rajesh Kumar Singh, Arun Devnath
